Title: Welfare of the people must come first amid oil crisis, says Selangor Sultan
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Sultan of Selangor, Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah, has expressed concern over rising global oil and gas prices, as well as the shortage of oil supplies resulting from the conflict in the Middle East.
His Royal Highness said the crisis had directly impacted Malaysia, causing oil and essential goods prices to rise and driving up the cost of living.
In light of this, Sultan Sharafuddin commanded the state government to give full priority to expenditure on essential needs, thereby helping ease the burden on the people.
"All forms of wastage must be avoided, and every sen spent must fully benefit the people, especially those who are severely affected and in need of assistance.
"Our government must remember that the welfare of the people must always be the priority in every plan and implementation of state policy," he said.
The Sultan made these remarks at the opening of the Fourth Session of the 15th Selangor State Legislative Assembly here today.
His Royal Highness was accompanied by the Tengku Permaisuri of Selangor, Tengku Permaisuri Norashikin; the Selangor Crown Prince, Tengku Amir Shah; and Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari.
#kw: nst, news, Malaysia news, Selangor, sultan, Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah, Middle East, West Asia, oil, gas, price, crisis, cost of living, wastage
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Songkran run organiser faces action over water wastage, safety breaches
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Kota Kinabalu City Hall (DBKK) will take action against the organiser of the Songkran Sunset Splash Run for violating event approval conditions.
Commenting on the weekend event, which drew public criticism and backlash against the local authority, DBKK clarified that it was not directly involved in organising the programme.
"Any approval granted to the organiser was strictly subject to compliance with the stipulated conditions.
"Based on our observation, several aspects of the event implementation did not fully comply with the approved conditions, particularly involving the method of water usage, as well as cleanliness and safety impacts in public areas.
"Appropriate action will be taken against the organiser in accordance with existing laws and guidelines," it said in a statement.
DBKK also said the organiser had been instructed to carry out immediate clean-up works and ensure the area was restored to its original condition.
Yesterday, Karambunai assemblyman Datuk Ali Akbar Gulasan urged authorities to be more mindful when approving such events, especially those involving water usage.
"There are water supply issues in several areas in Sepanggar and Karambunai. With the current hot and dry weather, the situation is expected to persist.
"Although I was not at the event, it is understood that there was water wastage during the Songkran festival.
"I hope the organiser and local authorities will be more careful to avoid wastage when others are facing difficulties accessing water," he said in a Facebook post.
The organiser, KSRun Athletic Club, has issued an apology in a separate statement.
"All water used during the event was fully borne by the organiser and carefully planned as part of the event concept.
"At the same time, we acknowledge that in our efforts to prioritise participants' experience and create meaningful memories, we may have overlooked certain public sensitivities and perceptions. For that, we sincerely apologise."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] UM to act over 'soft porn' theatre performance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Universiti Malaya (UM) will take appropriate action over a theatre performance alleged to contain "soft porn" elements during a campus arts competition, the Higher Education Department said today.
Higher Education director-general Datuk Dr Azlinda Azman said the ministry had received a report from the university on the matter.
She suggested the incident may have stemmed from students being overly enthusiastic in expressing artistic elements, leading them to overlook cultural sensitivities and societal norms.
"I believe it was more a lapse in judgment and perhaps due to their excitement in showcasing their art, which caused them to overlook sensitivities.
"Whatever the case, universities must always remain sensitive. We see that young people, when creating art, can become overly excited and may not have any ill intent," she told reporters after a memorandum of understanding exchange ceremony between the Higher Education Ministry and Universiti Pertahanan Nasional Malaysia at the Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre.
Azlinda said institutions of higher learning must guide and advise students to be more mindful of boundaries in artistic expression.
While creativity should be encouraged, she stressed it must remain aligned with Eastern cultural values that underpin Malaysian society.
"These young people create without bad intentions, but out of excitement. It is our responsibility to advise and guide them so that sensitivities are preserved," she said.
She also reminded students not to misuse artistic platforms in ways that could spark public unease or offend societal norms.
Last Thursday, Universiti Malaya said
the performance had clearly breached the competition's guidelines.
Under the rules of the Karviter arts competition, performances must be free of negative elements, provocation, or content that could affect religious, moral, ethical and cultural values, as well as the university's reputation.
Investigations found that the scenes performed did not align with the original script, which had already undergone a vetting process – a clear violation of the competition's conditions.
The issue surfaced after a video went viral on social media showing an intimate scene staged in a university hall.
The university apologised for the incident and said it would not compromise on any breaches of programme guidelines or regulations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly couple killed, four including three soldiers injured in Temerloh crash
Article:
TEMERLOH: An elderly couple were killed, while their 13-year-old grandson is in critical condition after their car was involved in a collision with another vehicle at Km134 of Jalan Kuantan-Kuala Lumpur here yesterday.
Perodua Kancil driver Zulkifli Md Yusof, 72, was killed on the spot in the 5.25pm crash, while his wife, Radzuan Mohamad, 67, died several hours later while receiving treatment at the Sultan Haji Ahmad Shah Hospital (Hoshas) here.
Their grandson, who was seated at the rear, is seriously injured and is receiving treatment in the red zone at Hoshas.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Nasyim Bahron said the accident involved Zulkifli's Perodua Kancil and a Myvi carrying three soldiers aged between 22 and 27.
"Investigations revealed that the 22-year-old Myvi driver, who was travelling from Kuantan to Kuala Lumpur, lost control of the vehicle before it veered into the opposite lane and collided with the oncoming Kancil.
"Zulkifli, who was driving the Kancil, was trapped in the car and died on the spot. His wife died at about 8.55pm while receiving treatment at Hoshas," he said in a statement today.
Nasyim said the three soldiers, attached to the Kementah Camp, were treated at the yellow zone of Hoshas.
He said that the case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for causing death by reckless or dangerous driving, which carries a maximum jail term of 10 years and a fine of up to RM50,000 upon conviction, and licence suspension.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 68pct  Malaysian elderly have multiple chronic conditions, survey finds
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Diabetes, hypertension and high cholesterol are alarmingly prevalent among Malaysia's elderly, with a new national survey revealing a heavy and growing burden of chronic disease.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said findings from the National Health and Morbidity Survey 2025 (NHMS 2025) showed that 39 per cent of elderly individuals have diabetes, 73 per cent suffer from hypertension and 76 per cent have high cholesterol.
He said the survey also found that 68 per cent of the elderly have at least two of these three chronic conditions, while 30 per cent suffer from all three.
"These findings are worrying and underscore the need for a comprehensive strategy," he said at the launch of the National Health and Morbidity Survey 2025 (NHMS 2025): Elderly Health here today.
He said the survey also highlighted low levels of physical activity among the elderly, with 30.6 per cent classified as physically inactive and 12.8 per cent engaging in high levels of sedentary behaviour.
He said 45 per cent of elderly individuals are experiencing sarcopenia, while 60 per cent are at risk of frailty and 11 per cent are already classified as frail.
He said the NHMS 2025 findings are crucial in shaping comprehensive and responsive healthcare policies for Malaysia's ageing population, in line with the National Ageing Blueprint Malaysia, the Elderly Healthcare Service Action Plan 2023–2030, and the National Dementia Action Plan 2023–2030.
"In this regard, the Health Ministry remains committed to strengthening the national health agenda through strategic initiatives that support the well-being of the elderly while ensuring the sustainability of the healthcare system."
Dzulkefly also called on all stakeholders, including government agencies, healthcare practitioners, researchers and community leaders, to prepare for the challenges of an ageing population by 2036 and to work together to improve the quality of life of the elderly in Malaysia, with the goal of achieving healthy ageing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health Ministry sends medical, psychological support to Sandakan fire victims
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Health Ministry has mobilised aid for victims affected by a massive fire at Kampung Bahagia water village, including deploying medical and psychological support teams to temporary evacuation centres.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said although no fatalities were reported, the ministry is taking proactive steps to ensure the well-being of those affected.
He said frontline teams comprising counsellors, nurses, medical assistants and medical officers have been dispatched as part of mobile units to assist victims at the temporary relief centres (PPS).
"Although there were no casualties, this is certainly a matter of concern for us. We have been informed by our director that immediate support will be provided.
"Our first line of response includes counsellors, nurses, medical assistants and medical officers as part of the mobile team. They are already at the PPS," he told reporters here after launching the National Health and Morbidity Survey 2025 (NHMS 2025): Elderly Health here today.
Dzulkefly added that the ministry is placing particular emphasis on psychological support, noting that such assistance is typically deployed swiftly in disaster situations.
"We have also mobilised additional support, especially in terms of psychological aid. We are usually quick in responding to such needs."
Yesterday, it was reported that a pre-dawn fire destroyed about 1,000 stilt houses in
Kampung Bahagia
, with the number of victims rising to more than 9,000.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner George Abd Rakman said police received a report on the fire at 1.30am, and the area was declared a disaster zone by 4am.
Sandakan Fire and Rescue Station chief Jimmy Lagung said the blaze engulfed an area of more than four hectares.
He said low tide hampered efforts to secure an adequate open water source for firefighting.
"Besides seawater, operations utilised water from a tanker and a factory's pressurised hydrant. Four hose lines stretching 1,000 feet and 12 water jets were used to extinguish the flames.
"A Fire Rescue Tender from the Kinabatangan station assisted by deploying two hose lines and four water jets," he said.
Jimmy added that the operation concluded at noon, and the cause of the fire is under investigation.
Disaster management efforts are being carried out in coordination with the Civil Defence Force and other relevant agencies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sanusi set to make acting debut in 'The Keris'
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: Kedah Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Muhammad Sanusi Md Nor is set to make his acting debut in the film "The Keris", which is scheduled to reach audiences next July.
He said his involvement in the film was limited to a role as a panglima, a warrior commander, who rallies silat practitioners ahead of a bout.
He said the character carries elements of Malay heroism inspired by the fighting spirit of historical figures, aimed at stirring motivation among the younger generation.
"This is a new experience in my life. My character is a panglima who comes to give encouragement to the silat fighters, including one who will face a champion from abroad.
"The spirit of heroism embodied by Hang Tuah is brought to life in The Keris; my role is to bring that spirit to the young people involved in the art of silat. If you want to see me act, come watch The Keris," he said.
He was speaking when met after the launch of the poster, teaser and trailer for The Keris here yesterday.
Reflecting on his first acting experience, Sanusi acknowledged it was no easy feat, as it required deep immersion in the character.
He said he accepted the offer because the role aligned with his interest in elevating Malay arts and culture, particularly silat.
"Acting is difficult because an actor has to truly inhabit the script and the character. I am a real-life person, but when I tried acting, it wasn't too awkward.
"I accepted because it is related to Malay culture. If it were a different kind of role, I probably wouldn't have taken it, but because it involves a Malay warrior, I also didn't charge any fee. It's free," he said.
He said the medium of film could be harnessed to deliver positive messages to the younger generation while also introducing the country's cultural heritage.
He added that the involvement of crew members from various races and backgrounds, combined with the emphasis on Malay culture, made the film unique and likely to draw the attention of audiences.
"A lot of influence comes from films, including negative ones. So we use the same medium to bring positive messages and our culture to future generations.
"I hope this film succeeds and receives a warm reception from audiences across the country," he said.
On the possibility of continuing to act, he said he was open to considering offers if given a suitable role.
"If there is a good role, I am willing to consider it. Our lives are like a stage performance — the only difference is there is no script," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Malaysian peacekeepers stay cautious amid situation in Lebanon
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's peacekeeping contingent will continue to operate in Lebanon while maintaining a cautious posture due to prevailing safety conditions amid a fragile ceasefire.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the Malaysian Battalion (Malbatt), deployed under the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (Unifil), remains stable with no major changes in its operational environment.
"Malbatt's condition remains stable and under control, with no significant developments.
"Although there is a ceasefire, there are times when violations occur, and that becomes an issue," he said after launching the Defence Ministry's pavilion at Defence Services Asia (DSA) and National Security Asia (NATSEC) 2026.
He said Malaysian peacekeepers would continue to carry out their duties based on prevailing security conditions, with operations adjusted accordingly.
"When conditions permit and the ceasefire is fully observed, they will continue operations as usual. However, if there are violations, they may remain within their base for safety reasons," he said.
Khaled said the armed forces had consistently reminded personnel to prioritise safety, given the unpredictable nature of the conflict environment.
He added that Malaysia's deployment location offered a strategic advantage, as its troops are not positioned directly along the volatile Lebanon-Israel border, where tensions remain highest.
"Our position is relatively better because we are not right at the Lebanon-Israel border. That area is mainly occupied by Indonesian forces," he said.
However, he cautioned that risks persist due to the evolving nature of modern warfare.
"I do not deny that with the use of missiles and rockets, threats can extend beyond immediate conflict zones. That is why we must always remain cautious," he said.
Recent incidents involving other UN contingents have underscored these risks.
According to BBC News, a French peacekeeper serving under Unifil was killed, while three others were wounded after a patrol came under small-arms fire in southern Lebanon, in what officials described as a deliberate attack.
The report said two of the injured were in serious condition and receiving treatment in Beirut, while another sustained moderate injuries. The peacekeeper who was killed is expected to be repatriated to Paris.
French President Emmanuel Macron blamed the attack on Hezbollah, although the Iran-backed group denied any involvement.
The incident comes amid heightened tensions in southern Lebanon following renewed fighting between Hizbollah and Israel earlier this month, highlighting the increasingly volatile conditions faced by peacekeepers in the region.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly living alone rises to 18.8pct, threefold increase since 2018
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The National Health and Morbidity Survey 2025 (NHMS 2025) has revealed that 18.8 per cent of elderly individuals in the country are living alone, nearly tripling from 6.3 per cent in 2018.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad, however, said 61.3 per cent of this group still report having strong social support despite facing systemic challenges, including limited mobility and financial constraints.
"With the elderly population now reaching 4.1 million and nearly one-fifth living alone, transportation limitations have become a major barrier to consistent access to healthcare.
"This is not merely a logistical issue, but a critical factor affecting their quality of life and safety," he said at the launch of the National Health and Morbidity Survey 2025 (NHMS 2025): Elderly Health here today.
Dzulkefly said the second major challenge faced by the elderly is financial burden, including that borne by caregivers, with one in three reported to be under severe strain encompassing physical, emotional and financial stress.
"These constraints are worsening given that only 40 per cent of Malaysians have sufficient Employees Provident Fund (EPF) savings for retirement.
[[nid:1421949]]
"This economic dilemma forces families to prioritise basic needs, often at the expense of healthcare," he said.
In terms of mental health, he said the rate of depression among the elderly has declined to 8.0 per cent from 11.2 per cent in 2018.
He added that physical dependency in daily living activities has also shown a decline.
"However, only 14.7 per cent of the elderly in the country are classified as ageing well, encompassing social, cognitive, psychological and physical aspects."
Dzulkefly also said that a significant rise in non-communicable diseases (NCDs), with 73 per cent of elderly individuals suffering from hypertension, 76 per cent from high cholesterol and 39 per cent from diabetes.
He said 68 per cent have at least two chronic conditions, while 30 per cent suffer from all three.
"The findings also show that 30.6 per cent of the elderly are physically inactive, contributing to 45 per cent experiencing sarcopenia, while another 60 per cent are at risk of frailty."
Dzulkefly said the NHMS 2025 report goes beyond statistics, reflecting the realities faced by the elderly and serving as a strategic guide for the Health Ministry.
"This data serves as both a moral and strategic compass in monitoring the implementation of the Elderly Healthcare Service Action Plan 2023–2030.
"Malaysia is expected to become an ageing nation by 2036, with more than 15 per cent of its population aged 60 and above.
"This pace is among the fastest in the world, and we do not have the luxury of time. We must act now."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DNeX unit bags services, infrastructure contracts for Saudi Makkah Route initiative
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Dagang NeXchange Bhd's (DNeX) wholly owned subsidiary Dagang Net Technologies Sdn Bhd has secured site facilitation, logistics and telecommunications infrastructure service contracts to continue supporting Saudi Arabia's Makkah Route initiative for this year's hajj season.
In a statement today, DNeX said the contracts cover operations across seven international airports in Malaysia, Indonesia and Türkiye, marking an expansion from the previous year with the addition of a new site in Indonesia.
"DNeX has been involved in the Makkah Route programme since 2019.
"The initiative, led by Saudi Arabia's Ministry of Interior under the Pilgrim Experience Programme, enables pilgrims to complete immigration pre-clearance in their home countries, improving arrival efficiency and overall pilgrim experience," it added.
Group chief executive officer Faizal Sham Abu Mansor said the retention and expansion of DNeX's role reflects strong long-term partnerships and recurring revenue potential.
"Our continued appointment across multiple international sites, together with the addition of a new location this year, underscores the trust placed in DNeX to deliver mission-critical infrastructure at scale," he said.
DNeX said for the 2026 hajj season, DNeX's scope covers key aviation gateways including KLIA Terminal 1 (Malaysia), Soekarno-Hatta, Juanda, Adi Soemarmo and Sultan Hasanuddin airports (Indonesia), as well as Istanbul Airport and Ankara Esenboğa Airport (Türkiye).
It continues to collaborate closely with Elm Company, a Saudi government-owned entity under the Public Investment Fund and a leading digital solutions provider supporting the kingdom's digital transformation initiatives.
Beyond the Makkah Route programme, DNeX is actively exploring broader commercial opportunities with Elm Company in areas such as transportation and security, with the aim of exporting its government technology and infrastructure expertise to new international markets, said the company.
"With seven global sites out of 16 secured this year, compared to six in the previous hajj season, DNeX remains optimistic about further expansion in future cycles, supporting the group's strategy to grow its recurring international project base and enhance long-term shareholder value," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azalina: Corporate manslaughter law could go beyond fines to social penalties
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The government is considering financial compensation and social responsibility measures as part of a proposed corporate manslaughter law, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said.
She said the Criminal Law Reform Committee is conducting a comprehensive review of the proposal, including the possible establishment of a tribunal to ensure appropriate and realistic sentencing.
Azalina acknowledged concerns that justice may not be served if perpetrators are unable to pay compensation.
"When a company is responsible, we cannot simply imprison the company although its board members could be held liable.
"One aspect being considered is compensation, whether from the company or the driver involved, as part of a broader framework. The tribunal may also make adjustments, as in some countries, compensation is not necessarily limited to monetary payment and could involve social responsibility measures," she said, citing past fatal bus accidents.
She also pointed to examples of anti-bullying tribunals, where those involved may instead be required to demonstrate good behaviour or undertake community service.
No decision has been made on the proposed law, as input from the Attorney-General is still pending, she added.
Azalina was speaking after an event marking the office handover ceremony for the Public Defence Division, the launch of the Justice on Wheels (JoW) initiative, and the accreditation of Legal Aid Department mediation officers.
The proposal was
first raised following the Gerik bus crash,
which killed 15 university students.
She said the law could be introduced either through amendments to the Penal Code or via a new act, similar to the United Kingdom Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man charged with possessing 1,451 litres of diesel without licence
Article:
BUTTERWORTH: A man was charged in the Sessions Court here today with possessing 1,451 litres of diesel without a licence.
Muhammad Zahirafandi Abu Bakar, 30, claimed trial to the charge, which was read to him by the court interpreter before Judge Roslan Hamid.
Zahirafandi is alleged to have been in possession of a controlled item under circumstances that give rise to reasonable suspicion at the Seberang Jaya police station at about 10.45pm on April 16.
The offence is under Regulation 3(1) of the Control of Supplies Regulations 1974, namely dealing in controlled goods, whether wholesale or retail, without a licence.
According to the charge, the diesel, amounting to 1,451 litres, was stored in six intermediate bulk containers (IBCs) at the rear of a white Dongfeng canvas lorry bearing registration number PLR477.
The charge was framed under Section 21 of the Control of Supplies Act 1961, which is punishable under Section 22(1) of the same Act.
If convicted, he faces a fine of up to RM1 million, or imprisonment of up to three years, or both. For a second or subsequent offence, the penalty is a fine of up to RM2 million, or imprisonment of up to five years, or both.
Zahirafandi said he understood the charge and the penalty, but pleaded not guilty when asked for his plea.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry prosecuting officer Alsad Nasrullah asked the court to set bail at RM10,000 with one local surety. He also requested an additional condition for the accused to report to the ministry office periodically.
"We also ask the court to ensure that Zahirafandi does not involve himself in similar activities throughout the duration of his case," he said.
In mitigation, Zahirafandi requested for lower bail.
"Saya minta kurangkan (I request for lower bail)," he told the court.
Judge Roslan Hamid then set bail at RM10,000 with one surety.
He also ordered Zahirafandi to report to the ministry office on the 20th of every month until the conclusion of the case.
He fixed May 22 for mention pending submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rice reserve stock increased to 300,000 metric tonnes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government has increased the country's rice reserve stock to 300,000 metric tonnes as an early measure against potential supply disruptions arising from the global energy crisis.
Agriculture and Food Security Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu said the move aims to ensure rice supply remains sufficient and secure, while safeguarding the basic needs of the people.
"The government is constantly monitoring the situation and continues to work hard to ensure everyone's basic needs are well taken care of. At the same time, Bang Mat wants to invite all citizens to continue practising thrift and avoid wastage," he said.
"All these efforts are to ensure the nation's food security remains strong, and that every citizen continues to enjoy sufficient and secure supplies," he added in a Facebook post today.
On March 13, Mohamad was reported as saying that the government agreed to increase the national rice reserve stock to cover a period of nine months, as a precautionary step to ensure food supply security amid geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two brothers killed in rain-triggered three-vehicle crash in Batu Pahat
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A rain-soaked stretch of road turned deadly when a car skidded into oncoming traffic, triggering a three-vehicle pile-up that killed two brothers and left several others injured at Km36.7, Jalan Kluang, Ayer Hitam, yesterday.
The deceased brothers, aged 20 and 30, were travelling in a car with two other passengers who are also their siblings.
Their 29-year-old brother, who was driving, and another sibling, 25, sustained severe injuries and were rushed to Enche' Besar Hajjah Khalsom Hospital.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Shahrulanuar Mushaddat Abdullah Sani said the crash occurred at 4.30pm amid heavy rain, when the brothers' car, travelling from Kluang towards Ayer Hitam, is believed to have lost control before veering into the opposite lane.
The vehicle collided head-on with an oncoming car and a sport utility vehicle (SUV), creating a mangled pile-up that brought traffic to a standstill.
"In the Kluang-bound car were four men who are siblings aged between 20 and 30. Two of them, aged 20 and 30, suffered severe head injuries and were pronounced dead at the scene," he said in a statement today.
The 42-year-old woman driving the oncoming car sustained minor injuries, while the SUV driver, 35, and his wife also escaped with minor injuries.
Preliminary investigations revealed the driver, who was travelling with his brothers, lost control of his vehicle in wet conditions, leading to the crash.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for causing death by reckless or dangerous driving.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man with dwarfism dies after heart complications in Kuantan
Article:
KUANTAN: A 26-year-old man with dwarfism, Mohd Zulhanif Abd Halim, died at 10.15am today at Hospital Tengku Ampuan Afzan (HTAA) after being placed in an induced coma for three days.
His father, Abd Halim Sulaiman, 49, said his son died due to heart complications after four days of treatment at HTAA.
"His condition was not critical when he was first admitted. He was still able to talk and eat, but on Monday night he was sedated after his oxygen level dropped significantly due to serious heart complications, causing his face to turn bluish.
"His condition worsened as he experienced breathing difficulties. His airway was very small and likely blocked, making surgery not possible," he said when contacted.
Abd Halim said he and his wife were able to speak briefly with their son before he was sedated.
"We accept his passing. We had already lost another child, Mohd Zulhanif's sibling, Zurin Hazlien, who also had dwarfism, in April last year.
"They were both the joy of our household, and now they have both left us. It has been a very painful loss over the past year," he said, adding that his son had been unwell for the past three months.
The remains will be taken to Masjid Sungai Miang for funeral arrangements before burial at the Sungai Miang Muslim cemetery, next to his late sibling.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's edge in the global circular economy
Article:
LETTERS:
AS the war between the United States and Iran fast approaches the drawn-out peace negotiations stage, the definition of a strategic material is rapidly expanding.
What we are witnessing is not merely a geopolitical conflict, but a live stress test of global supply chains, one that in some respects is beginning to rival the disruption experienced during the Covid-19 pandemic.
The obvious starting point remains energy. Oil and gas are not just fuels; they are feedstocks for petrochemicals that shape everything from packaging to pharmaceuticals.
Disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz have once again highlighted how geographically concentrated and vulnerable these resources are.
When chokepoints tighten, the ripple effects extend far beyond crude oil. Polymers, industrial chemicals and intermediate goods begin to feel the strain.
This raises an uncomfortable but necessary question. What happens if other arteries of global trade, such as the Red Sea or the Suez Canal, are similarly disrupted?
The answer is that materials once considered mundane, like plastics, paper and scrap metals, quickly become critical inputs whose availability determines whether factories run or stall.
In this evolving landscape, Malaysia finds itself in a quietly advantageous position. The country has long been an importer of post-industrial materials, particularly plastics, for recycling and reprocessing.
What was once seen as a low-value segment of the economy is now emerging as a strategic asset. As virgin material prices rise and environmental, social and governance requirements tighten, manufacturers worldwide are scrambling to secure reliable sources of recycled inputs.
This is where ESG and the Sustainable Development Goals intersect directly with trade and industry.
Destination markets such as the European Union are imposing higher and continuously evolving standards on materials, traceability and recycled content.
Compliance is no longer an option; it is a prerequisite for market access. At the same time, global consumers are increasingly discerning.
They expect products that are not only competitively priced and of high quality, but also aligned with environmental responsibility and long-term sustainability.
This demand is cascading down supply chains and reshaping procurement decisions at every level.
Plastics are only the beginning. Paper fibres, ferrous and non-ferrous metals, and even critical minerals are seeing renewed interest in recycling as a means of supply security.
Yet Malaysia's greatest gap lies not in processing capacity, but in domestic recycling rates. Collection systems remain uneven, and the culture of recycling has yet to reach the maturity seen in countries such as Japan and South Korea.
This is not an insurmountable challenge. Cultural shifts can happen quickly, especially when driven by younger generations. The ministries of Education and Higher Education have a role to play as active nodes in a national recycling ecosystem.
Recycling should no longer be framed as a good to have, but as a strategic national project tied to economic resilience and global competitiveness.
There are also misconceptions that must be addressed. Imported recyclable materials are often cleaner than domestic waste streams, with contamination rates typically below five per cent and often closer to three per cent.
By contrast, post-consumer plastics within Malaysia tend to have higher contamination levels. Standards and enforcement, including those aligned with Sirim, are critical in maintaining quality and credibility in the sector.
Malaysia should take pride in its role within the global circular economy. This is not a peripheral activity. It sits at the intersection of manufacturing, sustainability and the knowledge economy.
With the right policies, the country can attract investment, develop talent and position itself as a hub for material innovation where sustainability meets profitability.
Geography further strengthens this proposition. Malaysia's access to both the Indian and Pacific Oceans, combined with overland connectivity to China and mainland Southeast Asia, places it in a uniquely strategic position.
In a world increasingly defined by disruptions, countries that can secure both supply chains and market access will have the decisive edge.
NORDIN ABDULLAH
Founding Chairman, Malaysia Global Business Forum
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 10,000 Penang consumers spared water cuts during Sungai Prai pipeline works
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: About 10,000 water consumers in Seberang Prai Utara (SPU) and Seberang Prai Tengah (SPT) will not face supply disruptions during final connection works for the third Sungai Prai River-Crossing Pipeline (3rd SP-RCP), scheduled from April 24 to 26.
Penang Water Supply Corporation chief executive officer Datuk K. Pathmanathan said the three-day exercise would be carried out using "hot tapping" and "line stopping" technologies at Kampung Bagan Serai in SPU.
He said the method allows engineers to connect the new pipeline without shutting down the existing 975mm main pipeline, which channels treated water from the Sungai Dua Water Treatment Plant to key areas in SPU and SPT.
"This means consumers in Bagan Lalang, Jalan Kerapu, Mak Mandin and Sungai Dua in SPU, as well as the Prai Industrial Area in SPT, will continue to receive uninterrupted water supply throughout the works," he said.
Pathmanathan noted this would be the second time such technology is used in Penang, following
its successful deployment in January
during the final connection of the second SP-RCP near Sungai Dua.
"That exercise marked the first instance in Penang where a major pipeline connection was completed without a scheduled water cut," he added.
He said the success prompted Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow to direct PBAPP to adopt the approach for similar projects where feasible.
Despite higher costs – estimated at RM600,000 compared with about RM80,000 for conventional methods – Pathmanathan said the benefits outweighed the expense.
"Using these technologies avoids a scheduled water disruption that could have affected consumers, including industrial users in Mak Mandin and the Prai Industrial Area, for between 48 and 72 hours," he said.
As an added safeguard, the works have been scheduled over the weekend to minimise impact.
While no issues are anticipated, consumers in affected areas are advised to report any disruptions via PBAPP's 24-hour call centre or WhatsApp Business line at 04-255 8255.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: BHEUU should be elevated to full ministry, says Azalina
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said has called for the Legal Affairs Division (BHEUU) to be elevated into a standalone ministry, instead of remaining under the Prime Minister's Department.
She said establishing a dedicated ministry would enable the division to operate more effectively and deliver better services to the people.
"I hope that next year, the prime minister, if he remains in office, will fulfil his post-election promise to elevate the BHEUU into a full-fledged ministry.
"That is my hope and prayer. I cannot say much about it now, as it would still take about another year.
"However, my concern is that there is little point in having such a strong Legal Aid Department, with a capable Director-General of BHEUU and a competent Director-General of Insolvency, a good Director-General of Legal Aid, all placed under the Prime Minister's Department.
"It is not that the Prime Minister's Department is unimportant; rather, I mean that having a dedicated ministry—with its own secretary-general, divisional secretaries, and director-generals as we have today—would enable it to operate more effectively in serving the people," she said in her speech at the handover ceremony for the Public Defence Division, the launch of the Justice on Wheels (JoW) initiative, and the accreditation of Legal Aid Department mediation officers here today.
She said law ministry would demonstrate the importance of the legal framework as a critical arm of government.
"I hope I will not lose my job for strongly advocating the establishment of this ministry.
"But for me, if we want to do our best, then we must do it properly, right?
"What is the point of making promises if they are not fulfilled for the people?" she asked.
She said those aged 60 and above, particularly retirees including retired judges from the High Court or Sessions Court, are welcome to serve as mediators.
She expressed hope the Justice on Wheels initiative would be expanded to all parliamentary constituencies in the future, regardless of whether they are represented by the opposition or the government.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Local investors comprise RM48.9bil, 58.3pct of Selangor investments in 2025: Sultan Sharafuddin
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Sultan of Selangor Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah expressed his happiness that more than half of the investment value in Selangor, totalling RM48.9 billion in 2025 or 58.3 per cent, in 2025 was from local investors.
The Sultan of Selangor said he was informed that total investment in Selangor last year amounted to RM83.9 billion, exceeding the target of RM65 billion, and that 61,311 job opportunities were created.
"The state government expects that there is still potential to expand the manufacturing and industrial sector, which contributes 21.6 per cent, through new investments, especially in high-technology sectors.
"Therefore, I hope there will be new efforts to further boost the manufacturing sector activities that can offer job opportunities to my people," said His Majesty in conjunction with the opening ceremony of the first meeting of the fourth session of the 15th Selangor State Legislative Assembly for 2026 at the Selangor State Assembly today.
Meanwhile, the Sultan of Selangor was informed that the state collected RM3.02 billion in state revenue last year, an increase of RM154 million from 2024.
"The state government's achievements last year exceeded the original target, and I was made to understand that this year, even more encouraging achievements will be achieved with a target of RM2.8 billion," said Sultan Sharafuddin.
Moving forward, the Sultan of Selangor decreed that the issue of relative poverty should remain the focus of the state government in the Selangor Budget 2026.
"The relative poverty level has been reduced from 14.2 per cent in 2022 to 8.5 per cent in 2024. This must continue to be the focus of my government so that the people can live in better and more prosperous conditions in Selangor," said His Majesty.
As of the end of last year, the Sultan of Selangor was also informed that the state government had completed 75,493 affordable housing units, with 40,000 units targeted for construction this year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Sabah, Penang fans unite in song outside Likas Stadium [WATCH]
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A display of unity among football supporters has gone viral after fans from Sabah and Penang football clubs were seen celebrating together outside theY Likas Stadium here.
The 1-minute-23-second video, posted on Facebook by @HarimauMalaya, shows supporters from both sides joyfully jumping, singing and cheering together.
Accompanied by the beat of drums from a music band in the background, the fans appeared energetic as people around them recorded the moment.
The caption of the post read: "Ultras from Sabah and Penang met. Singing and cheering together."
Netizens praised the moment, describing it as a true spirit of football.
Facebook user Fahrin Ahmad commented: "This is the real football, entertaining together, not fighting."
Another user, @Nadz Sa, wrote: "Whoever we are, whatever our religion, whatever our race, but the sport of football can unite all of us, hope both teams can display a solid performance tonight. This is final bro."
User @Farhan YS Farhan added: "Very best from both supporters, this is what we hope for. In our homeland, no fighting when entering the stadium. Even though enemies during the 90-minute match, they still respect each other."
Meanwhile, @Soo Wai Lee said: "Happy, touched seeing supporters of two teams singing together. Malaysia is great."
The video has since gained traction online, with many calling for such unity among rival fans to continue both inside and outside the stadium.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IJM Land: Enjoy furnishing benefits with a move-in-ready or near-complete home
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Homebuyers looking at purchasing a brand-new property can now enjoy various perks thanks to IJM Land's new 'Settled' home ownership campaign.
The campaign features a selection of properties across Malaysia that are move-in-ready or close to completion, now with extensive financial and furnishing incentives.
Purchasing a move-in-ready or near-complete home allows buyers to see and select their unit, assess the surroundings and plan their living spaces without relying solely on floor plans.
For completed or nearly completed homes, the shorter timeline helps buyers save significantly on bank interest, avoid potential construction delays and gain much-needed financial clarity and certainty.
Investors also benefit by bypassing the years-long construction gap, enabling a faster transition to property listings and tenant profiling for an accelerated return on investment.
MOVE-IN SUPPORT
To simplify the transition for homeowners, IJM Land is offering furnishing benefits of up to RM427,000* and move-in starter perks worth up to RM160,000* with every home, depending on the specific property purchased.
*Availability subject to individual project offerings.
FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE
The campaign introduces several financial solutions to reduce the upfront burden on buyers:
• Zero per cent interest instalment plans of up to RM300,000*
• Renovation financing support of up to RM250,000*
• Home financing options of up to 120 per cent*
LONG-TERM SAVINGS
Selected units under the 'Settled' home ownership campaign also include waivers on essential living costs and legal fees. Key highlights include:
• Waived maintenance fees for up to 10 years*
• Free clubhouse membership for two years*
• Free internet subscription for one year*
• Free Sales and Purchase Agreement (SPA), loan legal fees, loan stamp duty, and Memorandum of Transfer (MOT)*
*Availability subject to customer profiles, credit scores, and individual project offerings. Promo ends 31 July 2026.
Available for a limited time, this offer covers selected units across IJM Land's developments, including:
Ambang Suria @ Alam Suria, Selangor
Austin Duta @ Johor Bahru
Avela @ IJM Rimbayu, Selangor
IJM Bandar Utama Sandakan, Sabah
IJM Ridge View @ Puchong
Riana Trees @ Pantai Sentral Park, Kuala Lumpur
Sutera @ S2 Heights Aman, Seremban 2, Negeri Sembilan
The Fairway Golf Villas @ Sebana Cove, Johor
The Terraces Condominium @ Bukit Jambul, Penang
Interested buyers can find more details and view the list of participating properties by visiting the official IJM Land
website
.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Naked man found wandering in Bayan Lepas, placed under psychiatric care
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Police are seeking to identify a local man who was found behaving erratically and naked along Jalan Lebuh Bukit Jambul, Bayan Lepas, here before being taken to hospital for psychiatric evaluation.
Deputy northeast district police chief Superintendent Lee Swee Sake said the incident occurred about 10.40am on April 16, after police received a public tip-off regarding a man behaving in an unstable manner by the roadside.
He said policemen were immediately deployed to the scene to assist a Malaysian Red Crescent Society (MRCS) ambulance team that was already attending to the situation.
"Upon arrival, police found the man in the middle of the road naked and behaving erratically.
"At a traffic light intersection near INTI International College Penang, the man was successfully detained with the assistance of ambulance personnel," he said in a statement.
He said the man was subsequently transported by MRCS ambulance to Penang Hospital, accompanied by policemen.
He said the man is currently being treated at the hospital's psychiatric ward.
"As the man did not carry any identification documents, police and hospital authorities are working to establish his identity and trace his next-of-kin," he added.
Police have urged those with information on the man's identity to come forward to assist investigations.
Photos of the man have since gone viral.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: All 58 MRSM to get full-time wardens next year
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Starting next year, all 58 Maktab Rendah Sains Mara (MRSM) will have full-time wardens among former uniformed personnel selected through a strict screening process.
Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the selection process will also require candidates to undergo special training to understand Mara's education philosophy.
However, he said starting this June, 18 MRSMs will first implement the warden service, before it is expanded to all 58 MRSMs next year.
"This follows the effectiveness of a pilot project launched last year at Balik Pulau MRSM and Besut MRSM.
"As a result, Mara has seen improved student discipline and a sharp drop in bullying cases.
"In fact, students show great respect to these full-time wardens as they are seen as their parents in the hostel.
"I see the combination of firmness and care used by these wardens as very effective," he said.
Asyraf said teachers would no longer be burdened with warden duties, allowing them to focus on teaching and student development.
"The reality is that teachers cannot monitor student discipline full-time, given their core responsibility of teaching during the day.
"Therefore, Mara will soon issue recruitment advertisements, and all candidates will go through a strict selection process and training to ensure they internalise Mara's educational philosophy and orientation," he said.
It was reported in August last year that MRSM had launched a pilot project placing former military wardens in student hostels as a key initiative to address violence and bullying in the institution.
The six-month pilot project was aimed at tackling bullying issues that must be curbed and breaking the cycle of violence.
Following the findings, a comprehensive study on the effectiveness of the initiative will be conducted with the cooperation of a third party, the Institute for Social Science Studies at Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor Sultan proposes postponement of 2026 Sukma [WATCH]
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Sultan of Selangor Sultan Sharafuddin Idris Shah has proposed that the state postpone the 22nd Malaysia Games (Sukma) in August until the conflict in the Middle East subsides and the Shah Alam Stadium renovation is fully complete.
Sultan Sharafuddin said the decision was in view of the high costs involved in hosting the multi-sport event.
"In the current economic climate, I am of the opinion that it would be more appropriate for the event to be deferred until the crisis in the Middle East is resolved and until the Shah Alam Stadium is fully completed," the Sultan of Selangor said in his speech at the opening of the Selangor state legislative assembly sitting here today.
Present were Tengku Permaisuri Selangor Tengku Permaisuri Norashikin, Raja Muda Selangor Tengku Amir Shah, and Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari.
Sultan Sharafuddin also decreed that the 2026 Piala Sultan Selangor be cancelled.
The Selangor Ruler, however, consented to going ahead with the Under-15 Silver Jubilee Cup football tournament involving school students from districts across Selangor.
"This Under-15 tournament for secondary students is aimed at nurturing young talent and instilling sportsmanship at the grassroots level.
"Moreover, football remains the most popular sport among the people of Selangor," Sultan Sharafuddin said.
Last week, the 2026 Sukma supreme committee announced that the event would proceed as scheduled from Aug 15 to 24 next year, despite challenges from the global energy crisis and the wider economic impact being felt worldwide.
Youth and Sports Minister Dr Mohammed Taufiq Johari said the decision was reached after careful and comprehensive consideration of all aspects, including preparations by the organisers and state athletes.
This year's Sukma is expected to feature 474 events from 37 sports, to be held from Aug 15 to 24 next year.
By gender breakdown, 239 events will involve male athletes, 217 involve women, and 18 are mixed events.
A total of 53 venues across nine districts in Selangor will be used for the competition, involving 12 local authorities, public and private higher education institutions, government and private agencies, as well as the National Velodrome in Negri Sembilan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pahang Sultan, Tengku Ampuan express sympathy over Kampung Bahagia fire
Article:
KUANTAN: The Sultan of Pahang, Al-Sultan Abdullah Ri'ayatuddin Al-Mustafa Billah Shah, and the Tengku Ampuan of Pahang, Tunku Azizah Aminah Maimunah Iskandariah, have conveyed their profound sorrow to those affected by the devastating fire at Kampung Bahagia water village in Sandakan, Sabah, yesterday.
In a post on the Kesultanan Pahang Facebook page, the palace said Their Royal Highnesses were deeply moved by the tragedy, which caused major destruction, with about 1,000 homes reportedly destroyed and a total of 9,007 residents affected.
"The royal couple extend their sympathy to all victims and hope they are granted strength, resilience, and ease in facing these difficult times.
"They also expressed their appreciation to all parties involved in rescue operations and providing emergency assistance," read the posting.
Their Royal Highnesses hope that recovery efforts can be carried out promptly for the welfare and well-being of those affected.
The Sultan and the Tengku Ampuan also prayed that all victims remain under Allah's protection and that the situation will return to normal as soon as possible.
It was reported that the fire, which broke out at about 1.30am, destroyed around 1,000 stilt houses in the water village.
Some
9,000 residents were affected
, although no fatalities have been reported.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia braces for oil price volatility, shifts to biodiesel push
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Malaysia is preparing for a prolonged period of volatility in global energy markets, with Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir cautioning that oil price stability at previous levels may no longer be realistic amid ongoing tensions and supply chain disruptions.
Speaking at the Monthly Assembly of the Economy Ministry, the minister said recent developments in global energy markets show that sharp price swings are becoming a norm rather than the exception, driven largely by instability in key shipping routes and geopolitical conflict.
"That is why we must be prepared for a new normal, where oil prices returning to US$65 per barrel may no longer be a reality but simply a memory," he said.
However, the government remains focused on structural adjustments to improve energy resilience, with the gradual expansion of biodiesel usage, moving from B10 blending levels to B15 in phases.
"Operationally, increasing biodiesel usage is feasible. By adjusting logistics and blending processes, we are confident in moving from B10 to B12 and B15 without waiting for major infrastructure upgrades," he added.
He also noted that Malaysia already has sufficient feedstock capacity to support the expansion, with palm oil production levels significantly exceeding current biodiesel demand requirements.
As such, the challenge lies more in operational adjustments and implementation efficiency rather than resource availability.
Malaysia's economic response must be both adaptive and data-driven, as global conditions have shifted into what is described as a "new normal" of persistent uncertainty.
In this environment, energy planning, inflation management, and structural reforms such as biodiesel expansion are being treated as interconnected strategies rather than separate policy tracks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ipoh city council assistant town planning officer fined RM3,000 for accepting bribe
Article:
IPOH: An Ipoh City Council assistant officer was fined RM3,000 by the Sessions Court here today after pleading guilty to accepting a RM2,000 bribe from a business owner two years ago.
Judge Meor Sulaiman Ahmad Tarmizi also ordered 53-year-old Syed Nazrudin Syed Mohamed Taib to serve one month in prison if he fails to pay the fine.
According to the charge sheet, Syed Nazrudin, an assistant town planning officer attached to the council's Town Planning Department Enforcement Unit, accepted RM2,000 in cash from 49-year-old Chong Yin Mei.
The payment was in exchange for ensuring that the council, as the local authority, would extend the business's premises vacation period from two months to one year.
The premises had allegedly committed an offence under Section 18 of the Town and Country Planning Act 1976.
The offence took place at NCS Craft Garden Landscape in Lorong Kuala Kangsar, Taman Merdeka, within the Kinta district, about 5.30pm on Aug 22, 2024.
The charge was framed under Section 165 of the Penal Code, which provides for a prison term of up to two years, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) prosecuting officer G. Nanthini prosecuted, while Syed Nazrudin was unrepresented.
During mitigation, the accused requested a RM3,000 fine based on plea negotiations filed with the MACC, which the prosecution did not object to.
The court also ordered that the RM2,000 bribe money be forfeited to the government.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MAGA figures finding it tough pushing Trump's campaign to grab Greenland
Article:
LAST December, Per Berthelsen celebrated a stressful year's end by taking his wife to a restaurant in Nuuk, Greenland's capital.
The 76-year-old parliamentarian and founder of the island's governing party is one of Greenland's most vocal opponents of Donald Trump, and had spent months campaigning against threats by the US president to seize his homeland.
After dinner, as the couple stepped outside for a taxi, a man appeared. It was Jorgen Boassen, the most prominent local face of the MAGA movement, sometimes referred to as Trump's "Greenlandic Son."
Boassen drew close and shouted that the two men could now fight, Berthelsen recalled in an interview. Just then, the taxi arrived and the Berthelsens left.
Boassen's behaviour sparked fury in Greenland's governing coalition after Berthelsen mentioned the confrontation on social media.
His aggressive behaviour, along with tactics adopted by a group of Americans – some associated with Boassen and all with ties to the White House – reveal how efforts to promote Trump's idea of taking over Greenland appear to be backfiring.
Besides Boassen, who has a record of physical and verbal assaults, the group includes a former US special forces officer; a man who contributed to Project 2025, the influential policy blueprint for Trump's second term; and an American chainsaw artist and biker who advised Kristi Noem during her time as Homeland Security Secretary.
This loose network of Trump allies has used a variety of tactics to spread US influence in Greenland and undermine Denmark, the kingdom that colonised the island of 56,000 people centuries ago and still controls its foreign policy and defense.
According to interviews with dozens of politicians and officials, these tactics include negotiating funding for a hallowed dogsledding event, cultivating relationships with opposition politicians, pledging big investments, and highlighting painful chapters in Greenland's colonial past.
One member of the group spearheading these efforts has coordinated his activities with the Trump administration and advised it on Greenland, Reuters has learned. A second told Reuters he provided reports about local politicians to White House staff.
The people involved in this network have previously been identified in media reports, but Reuters is revealing the extent of their coordination with the Trump administration, of their relationships in Greenland, and of the deep animosity their actions have sparked on the island.
"Those people, they don't have the respect," said Mute Egede, Greenland's foreign minister, of the efforts by these pro-Trump figures. "I don't respect people who don't respect that Greenland belongs to the Greenlandic people."
Pipaluk Lynge, chair of the Greenlandic parliament's foreign affairs and security committee, told Reuters she was "100 per cent skeptical of any other American" after the influence efforts she had witnessed.
"We are a colony. So we know what kind of tactics are usually used against people to take over a country."
In response to Reuters questions for this story, Olivia Wales, a White House spokeswoman, said the administration was "participating in diplomatic, high-level technical talks with the governments of Greenland and Denmark to address the United States' national security interests."
Boassen acknowledged accosting Berthelsen, saying he'd been provoked by Berthelsen calling him a traitor on social media. Berthelsen denied calling Boassen a traitor, but said he had criticised him in the Danish media.
While gaining independence from Denmark remains consistently popular in Greenland, Danish polling in Jan 2025 showed that 85per cent of Greenlanders opposed joining America.
That month, Trump said the US needed to control Greenland. He has since said that if the US doesn't occupy the island then China or Russia will.
Both Beijing and Moscow have dismissed the claims. The president's repeated comments about acquiring Greenland – and his refusal to rule out military force to do so – sparked months of tension between the US, Greenland and Denmark, and more broadly within the Nato alliance.
Ultimately, Trump backed off and the issue moved to a diplomatic track. In March, a US general told a Senate committee the administration was negotiating new bases in Greenland.
But last week Trump revived the issue, making clear he hadn't relinquished the idea of gaining control of the island. Furious over Nato's refusal to join the US-Israel war on Iran, he told a news conference on April 6 that the alliance was "a paper tiger."
"It all began with, if you want to know the truth, Greenland," he said. "We want Greenland. They don't want to give it to us. And I said, bye bye."
He followed that up with another anti-Nato blast a few days later, writing in a Truth Social post that, "NATO WASN'T THERE WHEN WE NEEDED THEM, AND THEY WON'T BE THERE IF WE NEED THEM AGAIN. REMEMBER GREENLAND, THAT BIG, POORLY RUN, PIECE OF ICE!!!"
Asked about Trump's remarks, a Nato spokesperson declined to comment.
Greenland's premier didn't respond to questions about alleged American influence activities sent to his office. Denmark's foreign ministry said it wasn't "in a position to go into detail regarding specific intelligence related cases."
FIGHT CLUB BAN
The readiness in Trump circles to embrace Boassen has shocked some in Greenland who view him as a menacing figure.
Allan Josefsen, a carpenter, grew up with Boassen in Qaqortoq, a town of some 3,500 in southern Greenland.
[[nid:1421854]]
Josefsen recounted that in the 1990s Boassen, then a teenager, approached him with two knives and stabbed him in the shoulder after Josefsen danced with a girl Boassen was interested in. Reuters couldn't independently confirm the incident.
Boassen, a bricklayer by profession, has repeatedly hurled verbal abuse at Greenlandic politicians in public.
Last year, while dining at a popular cafe, he approached two parliamentarians and called them "left-wing hookers" and "traitors" for their support of Denmark, according to Aqqalu Jerimiassen, one of the MPs and leader of a small party in the ruling coalition.
Boassen has also been banned from the Nuuk public pool, the main hotel in Nuuk, and the city's mixed-martial-arts fight club.
Boassen, who spoke to Reuters from New Orleans, confirmed the bans, which he said were for confronting people hostile to him or Greenlanders. He said he was banned from the fight club "because of my relationship with Trump."
A person connected to the hotel said Boassen was barred repeatedly for harassing or threatening patrons.
Boassen didn't respond to questions about the alleged stabbing of Josefsen and abuse of Jerimiassen. Reuters was unable to establish if Boassen has faced legal repercussions for any of his actions.
Boassen sprang to prominence in Jan 2025, when he guided Donald Trump Jr. on a visit to Greenland.
He met Trump Jr. at the airport and accompanied him to a waterfront statue of Hans Egede, Nuuk's Norwegian founder, who is viewed as a controversial figure by some Greenlanders for his role in the colonisation of the island.
Trump Jr. then hosted a lunch at Nuuk's Hotel Hans Egede, where a social media post shows President-elect Trump addressing a crowd of people in MAGA hats via speakerphone on a mobile device.
The visit enraged political leaders in Greenland and Denmark. Greenland is "not for sale," Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen said at the time.
Boassen, who has advised officials in the US, said his work is now financed by wealthy American donors whom he declined to name.
In February, Greenland's public broadcaster reported, Boassen spoke with Louisiana governor Jeff Landry, Trump's special envoy to Greenland, about problems in the island's healthcare system.
According to the broadcaster, Landry later noted the concerns to Trump, who announced he would dispatch a hospital ship to the island.
No such ship materialised, but Trump's announcement sparked another Denmark-US clash. Frederiksen said she was "happy to live in a country where there is free and equal access to healthcare for everyone" – an apparent swipe at the US healthcare system.
But Boassen had picked an issue with resonance back home, where the healthcare system has limited resources.
Denmark colonised Greenland in the 1700s. Since 1979, the island has become increasingly independent. It is now responsible for most governance, but Denmark still provides roughly US$700 million in funding each year.
In a Facebook post, Lars Erik Gabrielsen, a prominent mayor in Greenland's remote north, welcomed Trump's criticism of Greenland's health set-up.
"Our healthcare system is notorious among citizens for only handing out painkillers – that is nothing to brag about!!! The man is right."
Landry's office didn't respond to questions from Reuters.
Some Greenlandic leaders fear Boassen's loud support for Trump might create the impression that he represents the view of many locals.
"Even though they're small, they're loud," Jerimiassen said of Greenlanders like Boassen. "That's the concern for Trump's ears: that it looks like there are many for being taken by the US."
[[nid:1421853]]
'DEMONSTRATING FORCE TOWARDS US'
Boassen's main US contact has been Tom Dans. He contributed to a chapter on restructuring the US Treasury Department for the Heritage Foundation's Project 2025, which his brother Paul directed.
In December, Trump reappointed Dans as chair of the US Arctic Research Commission, a small federal agency, where he served in the president's first term.
Since then, two White House officials told Reuters, Dans has advised the administration on Greenland strategy.
A third White House official said Dans and his team were working to build support on the ground for Trump's plan. The official did not provide details.
Before that, Dans repeatedly met with National Security Council staff to coordinate efforts on the island, a former White House official said.
Boassen said he came to Dans' attention after Greenlandic diplomats told Dans about Boassen's support for Trump. Dans then hired Boassen to work for an NGO focused on "strengthening US-Greenlandic ties."
Sometime in 2024, Boassen and Dans turned their attention to Greenland's dogsledding association, which needed funds.
Dogsledding is an essential part of the culture in Greenland, where locals have used canine helpers to survive the harsh Arctic environment for at least 4,000 years. Manumina Lund Jensen, a dogsledding scholar at the local university, calls it "some of our soul."
Dans and Boassen met with Doris Jakobsen Jensen, then a Greenlandic parliamentarian and adviser to the dogsledding association. In her office, Jensen said, she and Dans discussed financing for the association's prestigious annual championship.
In exchange, Boassen told her that he and Dans would like the association to invite senior American officials to the event, Jensen recalled.
The association relies on a small government grant, with which it must fund air transport for dozens of racers and dogs, according to Jensen. After the meeting, she said, the association accepted an offer of American financing.
Dans wrote on social media that his NGO had raised US$250,000 for the championship. The association later said in a press release that some of its funding came from the US consulate in Nuuk.
The association declined to comment for this story. A State Department spokesperson said the US consulate in Nuuk covered the transport costs of dogs and racers for the 2025 competition, but "did not provide any funding to or through third party groups."
The spokesperson declined to comment on the actions of Dans and the other Americans who have been active in Greenland.
Boassen said the idea of inviting US officials was just a suggestion and that he and Dans had offered financial support after the association solicited the money.
In March 2025, the White House announced that second lady Usha Vance would attend the dogsledding championship. Greenlanders were outraged. "The only purpose is demonstrating force towards us," the then-premier said.
Vice President JD Vance subsequently said he too would travel to Greenland. With local anger rising, the Vances – accompanied by then-National Security Adviser Mike Waltz – visited an American military base, not the dogsled race.
In Jan 2025, Dans and Boassen were in Washington for Trump's inauguration with Kuno Fencker, a Greenland opposition parliamentarian, according to photos posted to social media. The three men visited the White House and attended inauguration events, the photos show.
Fencker didn't respond to requests for comment. Dans said the White House had instructed him not to comment on stories about Greenland.
THE MONEY TRAP
Some politicians in Greenland fear American investment offers might tempt locals to support a closer association with the US.
Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's former investment company has held a stake in a Greenlandic rare-earths mine, Securities and Exchange Commission records show. Lutnick divested from the company, which is now run by his sons.
Lutnick had "fully complied with terms of his ethics agreement, including all divestiture and recusal requirements," a Commerce Department spokesperson said.
Anna Wangenheim, Greenland's health minister, is concerned locals could be "lured" by promises of U.S. investment. "Our life standard is still very low," she told Reuters.
[[nid:1421852]]
Drew Horn has positioned himself as the most prominent champion of American investment in Greenland.
A former US special forces officer, he has built relationships with local politicians and dangled promises of investment in a Greenland that is independent of Denmark.
"If Greenland wants independence and inclusion in North America, the private funding exists to make it a reality," Horn promised on social media in 2025.
In a Fox & Friends interview that year, he said he had "the privilege of being one of the lead White House staffers" working to acquire Greenland in Trump's first term.
As Trump's second term got under way, Horn was in Greenland making promises of investment in the cash-strapped island.
Last year, he offered to invest in the company of prominent local businessman Bent Olsvig Jensen, Jensen told Reuters.
Horn said he was not working for Trump, Jensen recalled, but repeatedly paused during a meeting to respond to emails he said were from Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Commerce Secretary Lutnick.
Jensen said he didn't pursue Horn's offers to connect him to American energy and transportation companies. "There needs to be transparency in who is it you're dealing with – not some troll," he said.
Horn told Reuters he had never received nor claimed to have received an email from Rubio or Lutnick.
In February, Horn told another person over breakfast at a hotel in Nuukabout his interest in a mine in southern Greenland that had closed for several years due to a government-imposed uranium mining ban.
Audible from a nearby table, Horn wanted an exclusive agreement for its minerals, he said.
A female Greenlandic minister, Horn said, was secretly negotiating with his Greenlandic partner to ensure the permit for the mine "is turned back on."
Horn said his local partner was Svend Hardenberg, the former permanent secretary of the office of Greenland's premier. He did not name the minister.
Naaja Nathanielsen was at that time Greenland's minister for business and mining. She told Reuters she had never met Horn and, when told of his comments about the mine permit, said:
"No, I haven't had any discussions on that with anybody."
Hardenberg declined to comment. Horn didn't respond to questions about the mine.
Horn has also cultivated relations with Greenland's Naleraq opposition party.
Pele Broberg, Naleraq's leader, initially told Reuters he had only met Horn once at dinner with a Danish journalist and twice on the street. But photos shared with Reuters show Horn and Broberg also dining with their families at a restaurant accessible from Nuuk via a boat ride.
Told of the photographs, Broberg said he invited Horn on a day trip. In an interview, Horn said Broberg is "a personal friend" with whom he occasionally dines to "talk about ways to advance the interests of both countries."
In a later interview, Horn told Reuters he maintains "a dialogue" with White House officials, to whom he sent reports detailing Greenlandic political and business insights, including from conversations with opposition leader Broberg.
Broberg dismissed concerns by local politicians that Horn is seeking influence. He said Naleraq opposes giving Trump "an inch" of Greenland.
DIGGING UP DENMARK'S COLONIAL PAST
Some Americans have sought other ways to influence the local population, like highlighting painful chapters in Denmark's colonial rule of Greenland.
In March 2025, Chris Cox, an adviser to former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, arrived in Greenland.
The South Carolina chainsaw artist, whose group Bikers for Trump provided unofficial security at Trump rallies, was shown around by Boassen.
Following his trip, Cox appeared in conservative US media and said he had spoken to "hundreds" of Greenlanders to identify Danish historical injustices.
"The best thing that we can do really is try to help Greenland achieve some independence from Denmark," he said in an appearance on pro-Trump outlet One America News.
Cox outlined a litany of ways in which he said Denmark was mistreating Greenland, including having "secretly and forcefully given young girls and young women in Greenland birth control" to limit population growth.
Danish officials inserted birth control devices in thousands of Greenlandic girls and women, largely in the 1960s and 1970s.
In 2024, Greenland's then-premier called the campaign a genocide. Denmark apologised last year and offered compensation.
Cox also called into question the legality of Danish control of Greenland. In 1953, Denmark integrated the island into its kingdom without a vote by the population of Greenland.
In a separate interview, Cox asserted Danish "possession of Greenland is void and null, and this can be argued in court by our President Trump."
Cox didn't respond to questions from Reuters.
Berthelsen, the parliamentarian Boassen confronted outside the restaurant, believes Trump's threats and the influence efforts by the loose network of Americans have alienated Greenlanders, not won them over.
"The mental terror we have experienced from the United States, the present administration," he said, "has had completely the opposite reaction than the Trump administration hoped for."
* The writers producing this Special Report are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia Q1 trade hits record RM789.85bil, led by E&E exports [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's trade performance reached a historic milestone in the first quarter of 2026 (Q1 26), setting record highs for total trade, exports and imports, with strong momentum sustained through March.
The Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry (MITI) said Malaysia's trade in Q1 grew by 10.4 per cent to RM789.85 billion compared with the same period in 2025.
Exports climbed 12.7 per cent to RM426.53 billion, the second-highest quarterly level on record, while imports rose 7.7 per cent to RM363.31 billion.
This resulted in a trade surplus of RM63.22 billion.
"Notably, these were the highest ever Q1 values for trade, exports and imports," the ministry said in a statement.
Miti said export growth in the quarter was mainly supported by higher demand for manufactured products, especially electrical and electronic (E&E) goods as well as optical and scientific equipment.
It added that mining products, particularly metalliferous ores and metal scrap, also contributed to the increase.
"E&E products continued to anchor overall export growth, with exports increasing by more than RM40 billion, underpinned by sustained global demand and ongoing technological adoption across key markets.
"These products each attained their highest quarterly export values to date," it said.
For the month of March, Miti said trade sustained its upward trajectory, expanding by 9.3 per cent year-on-year to RM272.95 billion, supported by growth in exports and imports.
Exports rose 8.3 per cent to RM148.75 billion, marking the second-highest monthly value ever recorded, while imports increased by 10.4 per cent to RM124.20 billion.
"This resulted in a trade surplus of RM24.55 billion, sustaining Malaysia's streak of 71 consecutive months of surplus since May 2020.
"Trade, exports and imports reached their highest monthly levels on record for the month of March," it said.
The ministry said exports to major trading partners, including China, the US and Taiwan, recorded strong double-digit growth, while shipments to Asean countries saw a more moderate increase.
It added that exports to free trade agreement partners also rose, with higher shipments to Hong Kong, Mexico, South Korea, India and the UK.
"Notably, exports to the US, Taiwan, Hong Kong and Korea reached their highest quarterly values to date," it said.
Miti said escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East have increased volatility in global trade, pushing up logistics costs and affecting supply chain efficiency.
"Amid these developments, the external outlook remains mixed, with the World Trade Organization projecting global merchandise trade volume to grow by a modest 1.9 per cent in 2026.
"In parallel, Bank Negara Malaysia projected Malaysia's exports to grow by 8.6 per cent in 2026, while imports are expected to increase by 9 per cent, reflecting comparatively more robust domestic and external demand dynamics," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia inflation holds firm at 1.7pct as cost pressures begin to surface: Akmal Nasrullah
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Malaysia's inflation remains broadly contained despite a slight uptick to 1.7 per cent in March 2026, according to the Economy Ministry, signalling that price pressures are still largely under control for now.
